title: Simpsons Bible Stories

jokes.text: This is the hottest Easter ever. Even that praying mantis is losing it.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about the heat during church service
jokes.punchline: Visual of even insects suffering in the heat adds absurd escalation
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor with good visual component. The praying mantis detail is nicely specific and adds absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's so hot in here, I'm sweating like a pig at a bacon factory.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Homer, please. We're trying to have a service.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well turn up the air conditioning! Even the flies are passing out!

jokes.text: And as we pass the collection plate... please give as though the person next to you were watching.
jokes.setup: Traditional church collection appeal
jokes.punchline: Implies people are normally stingy when unobserved
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation about human nature in church context, perfectly economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Now, I know what you're all thinking: 'Why should I give when no one's watching?' Well, let me tell you, the good Lord is always watching. Always. Unlike me, I only check the collection plate on Sundays.

jokes.text: A chocolate bunny? Who put this wicked idol in the collection plate? - Relax. I found it in the Dumpster.
jokes.setup: Reverend treats chocolate bunny as religious transgression
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual admission he got it from trash deflates the drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - completely missing the point while being matter-of-fact about something gross. Good structure with religious setup and mundane deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Homer, you bought a chocolate bunny for Easter? Don't you know that's a pagan abomination?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I found it in the trash behind the kwik-e-mart.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's fine then.

jokes.text: Looks like God made you out of my sexiest rib.
jokes.setup: Adam commenting on Eve's creation from his rib
jokes.punchline: Homer's crude interpretation of the Biblical story
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer inappropriateness filtered through Biblical context. The 'sexiest rib' line is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So God took one of my ribs to make a woman? Why couldn't he just use dirt like he did with me?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And then he gives her to me and says 'Don't eat the apple.' Yeah, right. Like I'm gonna listen to God when there's a hot chick and forbidden fruit involved.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Best. Rib. Ever.

jokes.text: Hi-diddly-ho, paradise dwellers!
jokes.setup: God appearing to Adam and Eve
jokes.punchline: God speaking in Ned Flanders' characteristic style
jokes.explanation: Brilliant casting choice making Ned Flanders into God. The absurdity of the Almighty using 'diddly' speak is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-diddly-doodly, Adam and Eve-erino! I've got a real special garden-a-rooney for you folks. Now, there's just one tree you can't be eatin' from, okily-dokily? That there's the Tree of Knowledge, and if you go munchin' on it, why, you'll be in a heap of trouble-diddly-ouble, yes-siree-bob!

jokes.text: I can't believe you don't have a girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Adam commenting on God's relationship status
jokes.punchline: Treating the creator of the universe like a lonely single guy
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor treating divine beings with casual modern relationship advice.

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: So God, you're still single? I mean, you created the entire universe and all, but you can't seem to find a date?

jokes.text: I hate to be a bossy Betty... but I have to forbid you to eat its fruit. - And it would be even easier to avoid that temptation if I had a few extra wives.
jokes.setup: God politely forbidding the fruit with feminine expression
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately trying to negotiate for polygamy
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work. Homer missing the point of the serious commandment to immediately pitch polygamy is perfectly him. 'Bossy Betty' is great Ned-speak.

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Adam, you can eat the fruit of any tree in the garden, but I must ask you not to eat the fruit of that tree.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Can I at least have two wives?

jokes.text: And it would be even easier to avoid that temptation if I had a few extra wives.
jokes.setup: God forbidding the fruit and Adam agreeing to avoid temptation
jokes.punchline: Adam immediately asking for polygamy instead
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - agreeing to follow rules while immediately trying to negotiate around them

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: You must not eat from the tree of knowledge, Adam.

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: I understand, Lord. I shall resist all temptation.

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: But how about polygamy instead?

jokes.text: Let's call this little guy a groundhog. - Sorry, I already gave him a name: land monster.
jokes.setup: Eve suggesting a reasonable name for an animal
jokes.punchline: Adam's hilariously literal and unimaginative naming
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer stupidity - literal-minded naming system reveals his character perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Look at this beautiful creature. We should give it a name.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How about... Dog?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's a cat, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Then Cat.

jokes.text: Let's call this little guy a groundhog. - Sorry, I already gave him a name: land monster. - Really? Well, what do you call that thing on the branch? - Branch monster.
jokes.setup: Eve suggesting reasonable animal name
jokes.punchline: Adam's completely literal and unimaginative naming system
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic. Homer's brain-dead naming convention is hilariously consistent and shows his complete lack of imagination.

dialogue.speaker: Eve
dialogue.line: Why don't we call it a 'giraffe'? It has a long neck.

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: No, I'll call it a 'long-neck.'

jokes.text: You're pretty uptight for a naked chick.
jokes.setup: Snake trying to tempt Eve
jokes.punchline: Modern casual misogyny applied to Biblical Eve
jokes.explanation: Snake's modern sleazy persona applied to the serpent works well for character consistency, though the joke itself is fairly obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: Hey there, sweet thing. You know, you'd look a lot prettier if you smiled more. And those fig leaves? Total mom jeans. You should really do something about that body hair situation.

dialogue.speaker: Eve
dialogue.line: Excuse me?

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, you'd be way hotter if you tried. Also, you're crazy, right? All women are. That's why you can't handle the Garden without a man telling you what to do.

jokes.text: I think we should see other people.
jokes.setup: Adam needs to say something to defend Eve
jokes.punchline: Uses modern breakup line instead of standing up to God
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character work. Homer choosing the cowardly modern dating cop-out instead of Biblical drama is perfectly him.

jokes.text: I should have stood up for you during that whole 'Applegate' thing.
jokes.setup: Adam apologizing for not defending Eve
jokes.punchline: Referring to eating the forbidden fruit as 'Applegate' scandal
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay treating the Fall of Man like a modern political scandal. 'Applegate' as a name for the fruit incident is genuinely witty.

jokes.text: I love you even more than the butterscotch pond or the porno bush.
jokes.setup: Adam declaring his love for Eve
jokes.punchline: Comparing her to increasingly inappropriate Eden features
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from innocent (butterscotch pond) to inappropriate (porno bush). The absurdist Garden of Eden details are perfectly Homer-esque.

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: Eve, I love you more than life itself. You're more beautiful than the Garden of Eden itself.

dialogue.speaker: Eve
dialogue.line: Aww, thank you, Adam.

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: In fact, you're more lush and fertile than the Trees of Life. Your curves are as abundant as the four rivers flowing through Paradise. And your forbidden fruit... well, let's just say it's the most tempting thing in all of Creation.

jokes.text: Oh, what have they done to you, Gary?
jokes.setup: God discovering his unicorn has been killed
jokes.punchline: God giving the unicorn a mundane human name
jokes.explanation: The incongruity of a majestic mythical creature named 'Gary' is perfectly absurd and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Oh no! My unicorn! What happened to you?

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I'll just have to resurrect you as a human. From now on, your name shall be... Dennis.

jokes.text: I mean, how long can he hold a grudge? - Forever and ever and ever...
jokes.setup: Adam optimistically asking about God's forgiveness
jokes.punchline: Cut to Lovejoy reading about eternal punishment
jokes.explanation: Excellent scene transition joke - Adam's hope immediately crushed by Biblical reality

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So, Reverend, if I've sinned, God will forgive me, right?

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, that depends on the severity of your sins.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But He's forgiving, isn't He?

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Yes, but there are limits. Some sins lead to eternal damnation.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: *reads from book* 'And the wicked shall be cast into the pit of fire for all eternity, where there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth.'

jokes.text: And that concludes Genesis... the first of the 66 books of the Bible.
jokes.setup: Congregation clearly suffering through long Bible reading
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy announcing they've only finished one book out of 66
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy setup for the continuing Bible stories. The math of 1 down, 65 to go creates dread.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, folks, we've completed the book of Genesis. That's just one down, 65 more to go!

jokes.text: Kudos on your whipping. - Oh, he noticed!
jokes.setup: Pharaoh praising Willie's slave-driving
jokes.punchline: Willie's genuine excitement at receiving workplace praise for cruelty
jokes.explanation: Dark humor mixing workplace dynamics with slavery. Willie's childlike pleasure at recognition is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Pharaoh
dialogue.line: You drive those slaves harder than anyone I've ever seen. Magnificent work!

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Well, thank ye, sir! That means a lot comin' from you. I've been workin' real hard at bein' cruel and heartless.

jokes.text: Don't make me slay all the firstborn males again.
jokes.setup: Pharaoh demanding confession for pyramid damage
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to Biblical mass murder as minor threat
jokes.explanation: Dark humor treating Biblical plague as casual inconvenience for Pharaoh

dialogue.speaker: Pharaoh
dialogue.line: Who damaged my pyramid? Confess, or I will visit upon you the ten plagues of Egypt!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The ten plagues? Big deal. I've been through worse on a Tuesday.

jokes.text: That's the new omni-lash, boys. Just look at that snapback. - Yeah, it's sweet.
jokes.setup: Introduction of crueler whip for punishment
jokes.punchline: Treating torture device like sports equipment with technical appreciation
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor. The casual technical discussion about whip quality like it's golf equipment is brilliantly inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Willie, that's a beautiful whip. Where'd you get it?

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Got it from a fella down at the sports equipment store. Says it's got a reinforced handle and a triple-braided leather cord.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: That's some fine craftsmanship. The balance on that thing must be perfect for... uh... what exactly are you using it for?

dialogue.speaker: Willie
dialogue.line: Punishment, of course. It's got excellent snap and follow-through. Real professional grade.

jokes.text: Oh, now they're my people.
jokes.setup: Moses being asked to lead his people
jokes.punchline: Moses' sarcastic reluctance to claim responsibility
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Moses' reluctant leadership with modern sarcasm.

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Moses, I need you to lead your people out of Egypt.

dialogue.speaker: Moses
dialogue.line: Oh great, because I was really looking forward to a career in middle management. Nothing says 'fulfilling life' like herding thousands of complaints through the desert for forty years.

dialogue.speaker: God
dialogue.line: Will you do it?

dialogue.speaker: Moses
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, fine. But I'm not happy about it.

jokes.text: Oh, now they're my people.
jokes.setup: Others telling Moses to ask Pharaoh to free 'his people'
jokes.punchline: Moses' sarcastic observation about sudden ownership
jokes.explanation: Good Moe cynicism - pointing out how he's suddenly responsible when convenient

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Oh sure, I'll just ask the Pharaoh. 'Hey Pharaoh, how about you let my people go?' Since when are they MY people? I didn't even know these guys yesterday!

jokes.text: Bird, bird, giant eye, pyramid, bird. - Mm-hmm. Very good.
jokes.setup: Pharaoh dictating in hieroglyphics
jokes.punchline: Literal description of pictographic writing system
jokes.explanation: Nice visual gag pointing out the absurdity of hieroglyphic dictation

dialogue.speaker: Pharaoh
dialogue.line: Take a letter, secretary. I wish to tell my enemies what I think of them.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Yes, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Pharaoh
dialogue.line: Picture of me. Picture of them. Picture of me kicking them. Picture of them running away. Picture of me doing a little dance.

dialogue.speaker: Mrs. Krabappel
dialogue.line: Got it.

jokes.text: These are the juiciest frogs I've ever eaten. Ra has rewarded my cruelty to the slaves.
jokes.setup: Plague of frogs meant to punish Pharaoh
jokes.punchline: Pharaoh enjoying them as delicacies and missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of the Biblical plague. Pharaoh completely missing that it's punishment and treating it as divine reward is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Pharaoh
dialogue.line: Mmm, delicious frogs! Perhaps the gods have blessed me with a feast instead of a plague.

jokes.text: Do you think we could ever be more than just friends? - Not now, Moses! We gotta find a way outta here.
jokes.setup: Characters trapped in dangerous situation
jokes.punchline: Lisa picking the worst possible time for relationship talk
jokes.explanation: Good cringe timing with Lisa's inappropriate romantic timing during crisis, very in character for her.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, I've been meaning to talk to you about us.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, we're plummeting to our deaths!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I know, but I feel like we never communicate anymore.

jokes.text: Eh, slave labor. You get what you pay for.
jokes.setup: Shoddy construction causing deaths
jokes.punchline: Wiggum's casual economic assessment of slavery quality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum deadpan treating human tragedy like a business review. Excellent economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, at least slavery was good for the economy.

jokes.text: Screw this! I'm converting! Save us, O mighty Ra!
jokes.setup: Israelites facing the Red Sea with Pharaoh's army approaching
jokes.punchline: Bart's pragmatic religious conversion under pressure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart moment - abandoning principles for immediate convenience

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I'm Jewish!

jokes.text: Flush!
jokes.setup: Moses needs to part the Red Sea
jokes.punchline: Using modern toilet mechanism to drain the sea
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist visual gag. Reducing the miraculous parting of the Red Sea to a toilet flush is ingeniously irreverent.

dialogue.speaker: Moses
dialogue.line: I need to part the Red Sea.

dialogue.speaker: Moses
dialogue.line: Aha! I'll use this modern toilet mechanism to drain it!

jokes.text: Forty years? But after that, it's clear sailing for the Jews, right? - More or less.
jokes.setup: Bart asking about Jewish future after exodus
jokes.punchline: Lisa's uncomfortable acknowledgment of Jewish persecution throughout history
jokes.explanation: Smart dark humor acknowledging the tragic irony of Jewish history beyond Biblical times

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So, uh, what happens to the Jews after they leave Egypt?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, they wander in the desert for forty years, and then... well, throughout history there's been a lot of persecution.

jokes.text: But after that, it's clear sailing for the Jews, right? - More or less. Hey, is that manna?
jokes.setup: Bart optimistically asking about Jewish future
jokes.punchline: Lisa's uncomfortable deflection knowing the historical truth
jokes.explanation: Dark historical awareness humor. Lisa's knowledge of future Jewish persecution creates uncomfortable but effective comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Lisa, what's the Jewish future look like?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well Bart, I'd love to tell you it's bright, but... historically speaking, that's never really been the case.

jokes.text: The pie shall be cut in two. And each man shall receive... death.
jokes.setup: Reference to Solomon's famous baby judgment with pie
jokes.punchline: Homer escalating to death penalty for pie dispute
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection and escalation. Homer's violent overreaction to pie theft parodies Biblical wisdom stories perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All right, all right, I'll settle this pie dispute like the wise King Solomon. We'll cut the pie in half.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, you're STILL fighting over it? That's it — death penalty!

jokes.text: I'm not saying Jezebel's easy... but before she moved to Sodom it was known for its pottery.
jokes.setup: Classic 'I'm not saying she's easy but...' joke structure
jokes.punchline: Biblical city known for sin was previously known for innocent pottery
jokes.explanation: Solid joke structure mixing Biblical knowledge with classic comedy format. The pottery punchline is clever misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty the Clown
dialogue.line: I'm not saying she's easy, but Sodom used to be called 'Pottery Barn.'

jokes.text: No, it was his son, Goliath II.
jokes.setup: David thinking he already killed Goliath
jokes.punchline: Sequel villain logic applied to Biblical story
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic applying movie sequel naming conventions to Biblical narrative. 'Goliath II' is perfectly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, I already killed Goliath in the first movie. Why is he back?

dialogue.speaker: Goliath
dialogue.line: Sequel villain logic, kid. I got better. Also, I'm 20% bigger now.

jokes.text: And this time it's biblical.
jokes.setup: David vowing revenge on Goliath II
jokes.punchline: Action movie tagline applied to actual Biblical story
jokes.explanation: Great meta joke - movie cliche becomes literally accurate in Biblical context

dialogue.speaker: Homer as King David
dialogue.line: This summer... one man... one sling... This time, it's personal.

jokes.text: And this time it's biblical.
jokes.setup: David vowing revenge against Goliath II
jokes.punchline: Using 'biblical' as action movie intensifier while literally being in the Bible
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor. Using 'biblical' as slang for 'epic' while literally being in a Biblical story is very clever.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This time, Goliath, it's personal. I'm coming for you with biblical vengeance!

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: And so David prepared for battle, armed with nothing but a sling and a heart full of biblical rage.

jokes.text: Say, how's tomorrow for you?
jokes.setup: David about to fight giant with sling
jokes.punchline: Realizing he has no rocks and awkwardly postponing epic battle
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of heroic moment with mundane scheduling concern. The awkward politeness during mortal combat is nicely cringe.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, hold on a second. I need to get some rocks for my sling.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Um, can we... can we reschedule this? Maybe next Tuesday?

jokes.text: Without your precious hair, you no longer possess your fantastic strength! - That's Samson, idiot!
jokes.setup: David trying to defeat giant by cutting hair
jokes.punchline: Goliath pointing out Homer's Biblical character confusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - mixing up Biblical stories in the worst possible situation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll defeat you by cutting your hair!

dialogue.speaker: Goliath II
dialogue.line: That's Samson, you idiot. I'm Goliath.

jokes.text: Without your precious hair, you no longer possess your fantastic strength! - That's Samson, idiot!
jokes.setup: David trying to use hair-cutting strategy on Goliath
jokes.punchline: Goliath pointing out David has confused his Biblical stories
jokes.explanation: Great joke about confusing Biblical heroes. David mixing up different strongman stories is perfectly stupid and earned.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll use my hair-cutting strategy on you!

dialogue.speaker: Goliath II
dialogue.line: You're thinking of Samson, you idiot. I'm the giant you're supposed to hit with a rock.

jokes.text: Oh. I hope this doesn't get into the Bible.
jokes.setup: David failing spectacularly in battle
jokes.punchline: Hoping his embarrassing defeat won't be recorded in scripture
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-commentary on Biblical narrative creation. The irony of hoping embarrassment doesn't make it into the text we're watching is clever.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Hoping his embarrassing defeat won't be recorded in scripture

jokes.text: You're King David. I love you, 'cause you kill people. - Get yourself another hero, kid. I'm all washed up.
jokes.setup: Ralph admiring David for violence
jokes.punchline: David's defeatist response to child's bloodthirsty admiration
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ralph's disturbing hero worship and David's depression

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Wow, King David! You're like the coolest guy ever! You killed Goliath with a rock!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thanks, boy. But you know, it's not all fun and games. Sure, I killed a giant, but I also sent a man to his death just to cover up an affair with his wife.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Cool!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, Ralph, that's the whole point — it's not cool. I'm going to Hell.

jokes.text: Jonah! Oh! You died the way you lived- inside a whale.
jokes.setup: Ralph finding Jonah's body inside whale
jokes.punchline: Ralph's matter-of-fact observation about Jonah's lifestyle
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Ralph line - treating Biblical miracle as mundane character trait

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: He lived in a whale? That explains the diet.

jokes.text: Hope I don't give you... heartburn!
jokes.setup: David about to be eaten, delivering action hero one-liner
jokes.punchline: Weak pun about heartburn while being swallowed
jokes.explanation: Weak pun relying on obvious wordplay. The delivery during action sequence helps slightly but it's still pretty hacky.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I guess you could say this meal is giving me... heartburn.

jokes.text: Although I haven't seen his body, the blast that failed to kill me surely killed the giant.
jokes.setup: David claiming victory over Goliath
jokes.punchline: Backwards logic that his survival proves opponent's death
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic - completely backwards reasoning delivered with confidence

dialogue.speaker: Homer as King David
dialogue.line: I'm alive, so Goliath must be dead.

jokes.text: But Goliath was the greatest king we ever had. He built roads, hospitals, libraries. To us he was Goliath the Consensus Builder.
jokes.setup: Ralph presumably saving everyone by killing the giant
jokes.punchline: Revealing Goliath was actually a beloved progressive leader
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of Biblical narrative. Making the villain into a misunderstood progressive leader is brilliant social commentary.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: I'm gonna kill the giant!

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Ralph, no! That's Goliath! He was a beloved progressive leader!

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: Oops.

jokes.text: You're under arrest for 'megacide.'
jokes.setup: Ralph being arrested for killing Goliath
jokes.punchline: Wiggum's made-up charge for giant murder
jokes.explanation: Good Wiggum malapropism - inventing legal terminology for unusual situation

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You're under arrest for the murder of Goliath!

jokes.text: Eh, it's not the end of the world.
jokes.setup: Homer dismissing missing church service
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to actual apocalypse beginning
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic timing with Homer's casual dismissal immediately followed by literal end times. Great economy and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Church? Pfft. Nothing bad ever happens on a Sunday.
[Cut to: apocalypse beginning - sky darkens, ground shakes, chaos erupts]

jokes.text: Bart, are you wearing clean underwear? - Not anymore.
jokes.setup: Marge's maternal concern during apocalypse
jokes.punchline: Bart's admission of immediate pants-wetting from fear
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/punchline with good character integration. Marge's maternal instincts during apocalypse and Bart's honest response work well.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, with the world ending, have you at least been eating your vegetables?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, I wet my pants like five minutes ago.

jokes.text: It's the rapture! And I never knew true love. I never used those pizza coupons!
jokes.setup: Character listing regrets during apocalypse
jokes.punchline: Escalating from deep emotional regret to trivial consumer regret
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from profound to mundane regrets. The pizza coupons detail is perfectly Moe-like in its pathetic specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I wish I'd spent more time with my family instead of working at the bar.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I wish I'd told my mother I loved her before she passed.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I wish I'd bought that Betamax player when they were on sale.

jokes.text: Oh, they're out of hot dogs! And the coleslaw has pineapple in it! German potato salad!
jokes.setup: Homer appears to be screaming in hell's torment
jokes.punchline: Actually complaining about disappointing barbecue offerings
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment. Treating eternal damnation like a disappointing church picnic is brilliantly in character and subversive.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why?! Why?! Why?! This is the worst day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Devil
dialogue.line: Homer, you're in hell.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hell? Sweetie, I'm already home. I'm just upset about the barbecue at work today. They said it was going to be ribs, but all they had were hot dogs and hamburgers!

tmdb_overview: When Reverend Lovejoy subjects the congregation to an extended Bible reading, the Simpsons fall asleep and have dreams based on Bible stories.
insight: Four biblical parodies sustain 2.5 jokes per minute across 49 gags.
